text: :+1: Whole Code Catalog
:+1: Whole Code Catalog
So what is the Whole Code Catalog?
So what is the Whole Code Catalog?
Over the past year and a half, I've been collaborating with Dark to research programming languages and tools. The Whole Code Catalog is sharing those reviews publicly.

As I recall the Whole Earth Catalog was infatuated with Geodesic Domes, which were a disaster in practice. I wonder what is the Geodesic Dome of Code? Box and arrow dataflow?

Where can I learn about t2 tile? I'm looking for a quick summary or goals
Very interesting and promising! Thank you for sharing this!
Very interesting and promising! Thank you for sharing this!

<@U5TCAFTD3> I don’t have a quick answer for you unfortunately. Our home on the web is still Gitter which is more of an long form chat. There’s the [YouTube video series](<https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC1M91QuLZfCzHjBMEKvIc-A>) perhaps one of the early episodes can answer some things. There’s always <@UKLV35EEM> too.

T2 tackles building real hardware (the tile) that can implement some of the ideas and previous theory. (In no particular order):

* resistance to hardware nondetermism and faults, under arbitrary failure modes
* indefinite scalability
* robustness via artificial life principles.

Admittedly I’m not active with the hardware side.

attachments.title: The T2 Tile Project
attachments.text: Extraordinary computing demands extraordinary hardware. The T2 Tile Project is building a new best-effort 'unit' of computing called the 'T2 Tile'. Every wee...

text: I have a question. The versionning of types/functions/package/standard lib looks great, but does it scale well with lots of edits/versions? And what is the "size" of the biggest user's code base managed by dark?

I have a question. The versionning of types/functions/package/standard lib looks great, but does it scale well with lots of edits/versions? And what is the "size" of the biggest user's code base managed by dark?

Hi <@U5TCAFTD3>. The goal of the T2 tile project is to demonstrate a strictly indefinitely scalable computer architecture, and use the resulting system to set an initial benchmark record for the 'Average Event Rate' (AER) that such an architecture can achieve.  Perhaps the fastest way into the goals and concepts is the 2016 AAAI paper (5pg Q&A) at <https://www.aaai.org/ocs/index.php/AAAI/AAAI16/paper/download/11987/12203>
Our biggest users aren’t that big (15 tables, 60 routes), so we will definitely be figuring out scaling as we go.
Our biggest users aren’t that big (15 tables, 60 routes), so we will definitely be figuring out scaling as we go.
Thanks! I'll check it out

this is insanely inspiring. stop tempting me to move to SF!!

>>>Dark runs interpreters in the cloud. When you write new code in a function or HTTP/event handler, we send a diff of the Abstract Syntax Tree (the representation of your code that our editor and servers use under the hood) to our servers, and then we run that code when requests come in. So deployment is just a small write to a database, which is instant and atomic. Our deploys are so fast because we trimmed a deploy to the smallest thing possible.

this is insanely inspiring. stop tempting me to move to SF!!

>>>Dark runs interpreters in the cloud. When you write new code in a function or HTTP/event handler, we send a diff of the Abstract Syntax Tree (the representation of your code that our editor and servers use under the hood) to our servers, and then we run that code when requests come in. So deployment is just a small write to a database, which is instant and atomic. Our deploys are so fast because we trimmed a deploy to the smallest thing possible.

What I like a lot from reading this article, is that with dark, when you are editing something you actually modify the "running" system. And as the dark environment is aware of feature (why you modify) and version (what your modification is changing), it is safe to do it and test it "live" and then deploy it very quickly when you are confident with your modification. In fact, the system is centered on what your intention is (as a programmer) and you can focus on it and not on the tools, and the system is ready to commit your changes with ease.

> When you upgrade a package in Dark, we don’t just replace the use of every function or type across your codebase immediately — that doesn’t seem safe. Rather, all code continues using the exact same version that they were using, and you gain the option of upgrading uses of functions and types to the new version on a case-by-case basis using feature flags.

Isn't it onerous to manage so many feature flags? Does upgrading to a minor new version require patching every single call-site for the library?

> When you upgrade a package in Dark, we don’t just replace the use of every function or type across your codebase immediately — that doesn’t seem safe. Rather, all code continues using the exact same version that they were using, and you gain the option of upgrading uses of functions and types to the new version on a case-by-case basis using feature flags.

Isn't it onerous to manage so many feature flags? Does upgrading to a minor new version require patching every single call-site for the library?

<@UCUSW7WVD> Though this is a worry for sure. Theoretically, it would involve patching the callsite for every _changed_ function. So in a minor version, there would presumably be only a few different functions. My expectation is that you’ll have a command to change everything (using a feature flag) and then slow roll it. A bit like dependabot on github

<@UCUSW7WVD> Though this is a worry for sure. Theoretically, it would involve patching the callsite for every _changed_ function. <@U6BRN7A0Y> I feel the batch update dependency call site to the latest version might trigger the redundant dependency problem outline here: <https://hypirion.com/musings/a-resilient-git-dependency-algorithm> Have you thought about that problem or does Dark have a different dependency tracking mechanism that side steps this?
